status_notes: Initial research from AWS IAM documentation, 2026-04-05. Pass 1 (service docs) and partial Pass 2 (API). Needs Pass 3 (cross-reference) and Pass 5 (human review with live AWS environment).
notes: Foundation service — every AWS service depends on IAM for authentication and authorization. IAM is global (not regional). No charge for IAM itself. Security surface includes: user/role management, MFA, password policies, access keys, policies (7 types), Access Analyzer, permissions boundaries, SCPs/RCPs, and credential reporting.

match_nodes.description: Multi-factor authentication for IAM users and root account

data.description: Requires a second authentication factor beyond password for console sign-in and API operations. Supports passkeys/security keys (FIDO2, phishing-resistant), virtual authenticator apps (TOTP), and hardware TOTP tokens.

options.name: Passkeys and Security Keys
options.description: FIDO2 phishing-resistant MFA — physical keys (YubiKey) or synced passkeys (Google, Apple, 1Password, etc.)

options.name: Virtual Authenticator App
options.description: TOTP-based software token (RFC 6238) on phone/device

options.name: Hardware TOTP Token
options.description: Physical device generating 6-digit TOTP codes. Must be AWS-approved.

default.note: MFA is not enabled by default. AWS strongly recommends enabling MFA for all users, especially the root account.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use phishing-resistant MFA (passkeys or security keys) as the primary method. Register up to 8 MFA devices per user for redundancy.
data.limits: Maximum 8 MFA devices per principal (any combination of types). Only 1 device required to sign in.
data.recent_changes: SMS-based MFA has been deprecated. Users must migrate to passkeys, virtual MFA, or hardware tokens.

configure.console: IAM → Users → {user} → Security credentials → Assign MFA device
configure.note: FIDO security keys can only be registered via console, not CLI/API

match_nodes.description: Account-level password policy for IAM users

data.description: Configures password requirements for IAM user console passwords. Applies account-wide to all IAM users.

options.note: If true, expired passwords require admin reset

data.vendor_recommendation: Combine a strong password policy with MFA. Consider federation with an identity provider instead of IAM user passwords.

configure.console: IAM → Account settings → Edit password policy

match_nodes.description: Long-term credentials for programmatic AWS access

data.description: Access key ID + secret access key pairs for CLI/SDK/API access. Long-term credentials that persist until explicitly rotated or deleted.

options.note: Keys can be deactivated without deletion
Allows rotation: create new, migrate, delete old

default.note: No access keys created by default. Must be explicitly created.

data.vendor_recommendation: Prefer IAM roles with temporary credentials over access keys. If access keys are required: rotate regularly, never embed in code, use IAM Access Last Used to identify stale keys, deactivate before deletion.

audit.cli_all: aws iam generate-credential-report && aws iam get-credential-report
audit.console: IAM → Users → {user} → Security credentials → Access keys
audit.note: Credential report shows all users, access key ages, and last used dates

data.description: Security practices for the AWS account root user, which has unrestricted access to all resources. Root user cannot be limited by IAM policies, SCPs, or permissions boundaries.
data.vendor_recommendation: Enable MFA on root. Do not create access keys for root. Do not use root for everyday tasks. Use root only for tasks that require root credentials (account-level settings, billing, certain service operations).

audit.cli_root_keys: aws iam list-access-keys (when authenticated as root — should return empty)

configure.console: Root user → My Security Credentials → MFA → Assign MFA device
configure.note: Root MFA can only be configured via console

match_nodes.description: JSON policies attached to users, groups, and roles that grant permissions

data.description: Primary mechanism for granting permissions. Three subtypes: AWS managed policies (pre-built by AWS), customer managed policies (you create), and inline policies (directly attached to a single identity). Policies define allowed/denied actions on specific resources under specific conditions.

options.description: Pre-built by AWS for common use cases. Cannot modify. Updated by AWS.
You create and maintain. Reusable across identities. Full control.
Embedded directly in a user/group/role. One-to-one. Deleted with identity.

data.vendor_recommendation: Start with AWS managed policies, then create customer managed policies for least privilege. Avoid inline policies for most use cases. Use IAM Access Analyzer to generate least-privilege policies from actual usage.

match_nodes.description: Maximum permission limits for IAM users and roles

data.description: A managed policy used as a ceiling on the maximum permissions an identity-based policy can grant. Does not grant permissions itself — only limits them. Used for safe delegation: allow developers to create roles but limit what those roles can do.
data.vendor_recommendation: Use permissions boundaries to delegate permissions management within an account without granting unrestricted IAM access.

data.description: JSON policies that set maximum permissions for all IAM users and roles in an AWS Organization account or OU. Do not grant permissions — only limit them. Affect the root user. Require AWS Organizations with all features enabled.
data.vendor_recommendation: Use SCPs to establish permission guardrails across multiple accounts. Pair with identity-based policies to grant specific permissions within those guardrails.

match_nodes.description: Organization-wide maximum permission guardrails for resources (RCPs)

data.description: JSON policies that set maximum permissions for resources across organization accounts. Similar to SCPs but apply to resources rather than identities. Not all AWS services support RCPs.

match_nodes.description: IAM Access Analyzer — identify unintended access and unused permissions

data.description: Service that analyzes resource policies and IAM configurations to identify: external access (resources accessible from outside your account/org), unused access (permissions, roles, access keys not used within a tracking period), and policy issues (validates policies against best practices).

options.description: Identifies resources accessible from outside your zone of trust
Identifies unused permissions, roles, and access keys
Generates least-privilege policies from CloudTrail access activity
100+ checks against IAM JSON policy language and best practices
Validate policies against custom rules

data.vendor_recommendation: Enable Access Analyzer in every region. Review findings regularly. Use policy generation to create least-privilege policies based on actual usage.

match_nodes.description: Account-level report of all IAM users and their credential status

data.description: CSV report listing all IAM users and the status of their credentials: passwords, access keys, MFA devices, last used dates, rotation dates. Generated on demand, cached for 4 hours.

audit.cli: aws iam generate-credential-report && sleep 5 && aws iam get-credential-report --output text --query Content | base64 --decode

data.note: Report includes: user, ARN, creation time, password enabled/last used/last changed/next rotation, MFA active, access key 1&2 active/last rotated/last used/region/service.

match_nodes.description: IAM roles for temporary credential-based access

data.description: Identities with policies that determine what they can do, but instead of being associated with a person, roles are assumed by anyone who needs them. Provide temporary security credentials (not long-term). Used by: EC2 instances, Lambda functions, cross-account access, federated users, AWS services.
data.vendor_recommendation: Use roles instead of IAM users wherever possible. Prefer roles for workloads (EC2, Lambda, ECS). Use roles for cross-account access. Use IAM Identity Center with roles for human access.

match_nodes.description: Identity federation with external identity providers

data.description: Allow users from external identity providers (corporate AD, Google, Facebook, SAML 2.0, OIDC) to access AWS resources without creating IAM users. Users assume roles and get temporary credentials.

options.description: Recommended. Centralized access management with built-in IdP or external SAML/OIDC.
Direct federation with SAML identity providers
Web identity federation with OpenID Connect providers (Google, Facebook, Amazon Cognito)
For workloads outside AWS that need temporary credentials using X.509 certificates
For environments that don't support SAML/OIDC

data.vendor_recommendation: Require human users to use federation with an identity provider instead of IAM users with long-term credentials. Use IAM Identity Center as the primary federation mechanism.

match_nodes.description: Condition keys for fine-grained policy control

data.description: Policy conditions restrict when a policy applies. Global condition keys work across all services. Service-specific condition keys add granular control. Common security conditions: require MFA, restrict by IP/VPC, require encryption, enforce tagging.

options.description: Require MFA for the operation
Restrict by source IP address or CIDR range
Restrict to specific VPC
Restrict to specific VPC endpoint
Require HTTPS (TLS)
Restrict to specific AWS regions
Restrict to members of an AWS Organization
Restrict based on resource tags
Restrict based on principal tags (ABAC)

data.vendor_recommendation: Use conditions to further restrict access. Require TLS for all requests. Restrict by VPC/IP when possible. Use ABAC (attribute-based access control) with tag conditions for scalable permissions.

match_nodes.description: Information about when IAM resources were last used

data.description: Tracks when IAM users, roles, and access keys were last used to access AWS services. Used to identify stale credentials and unused permissions for cleanup.
data.vendor_recommendation: Regularly review last accessed information. Remove users, roles, permissions, and credentials that haven't been used. Reduce your attack surface.

match_nodes.description: Temporary permission limits passed when assuming roles or federating

data.description: Advanced JSON policies passed as parameters when creating temporary sessions. Limit permissions for that session only — intersection of role policies and session policies. Used via AssumeRole, AssumeRoleWithSAML, AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity, GetFederationToken.

options.description: Single JSON policy via Policy parameter
Up to 10 managed policy ARNs via PolicyArns parameter

match_nodes.description: JSON policies attached to resources that grant cross-account access

data.description: Policies attached directly to AWS resources (S3 buckets, SQS queues, KMS keys, Lambda functions, etc.) that grant specified principals permission to perform actions. Primary mechanism for cross-account access. Always inline (no managed resource-based policies).
data.note: IAM role trust policies are a special case of resource-based policies — they define who can assume the role.

match_nodes.description: Access Control Lists for cross-account resource access

data.description: Legacy access control mechanism used by S3, WAF, and VPC. Not JSON format. Cross-account only — cannot grant permissions to same-account principals. Less flexible than resource-based policies.
data.vendor_recommendation: Use resource-based policies instead of ACLs where possible. S3 recommends disabling ACLs (Object Ownership = BucketOwnerEnforced).
data.note: ACLs are being phased out in favor of resource-based policies and bucket ownership controls.
